claim: High cardiopulmonary fitness causes increased mortality rate.

result.claim: High cardiopulmonary fitness causes increased mortality rate.
result.reasoning: The claim that high cardiopulmonary fitness causes increased mortality rate is strongly refuted by the available evidence. Multiple propositions and chunks consistently indicate that *low* cardiorespiratory fitness is an established risk factor for cardiovascular disease (CVD) morbidity and mortality. For instance, proposition `prop_2048` from paper `13230773` explicitly states, "Low cardiorespiratory fitness is an established risk factor for cardiovascular disease (CVD) morbidity and mortality." The abstract of paper `13230773` further supports this by noting that "One consequence of inactivity, low cardiorespiratory fitness, is an established risk factor for cardiovascular disease (CVD) morbidity and mortality." No evidence was found to support the claim that high cardiopulmonary fitness leads to increased mortality.

evidence.text: BMI is a strong predictor of overall mortality.
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 20% higher respiratory mortality (HR 1.20 [1.07-1.34]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Patients with diabetes mellitus are known to have a high morbidity and mortality after an acute myocardial infarction.
evidence.source: Impact of diabetes on long-term prognosis in patients with unstable angina and non-Q-wave myocardial infarction: results of the OASIS (Organization to Assess Strategies for Ischemic Syndromes) Registry.

evidence.text: Low fitness was identified in 13.9% of adults.
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: Low cardiorespiratory fitness is an established risk factor for cardiovascular disease (CVD) morbidity and mortality.
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 60-120% higher diabetic mortality (HR 2.16 [1.89-2.46]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: The relative risk of death after heart failure diagnosis was 1.33 for men.
evidence.source: Trends in heart failure incidence and survival in a community-based population.

evidence.text: Low fitness was identified in approximately 8.5 million US adults.
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: The prevalence of low fitness was higher in adult females (16.2%) than in males (11.8%).
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: Participants underwent submaximal graded exercise treadmill testing to achieve at least 75% to 90% of their age-predicted maximum heart rate.
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: Low fitness was identified in 33.6% of adolescents.
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: Each 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was on average associated with about 30% higher overall mortality (hazard ratio [HR] 1.29 [95% CI 1.27-1.32]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 40% higher vascular mortality (HR 1.41 [1.37-1.45]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: The prevalence of low fitness was similar in adolescent females (34.4%) and males (32.9%).
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 20% higher mortality from all other causes (HR 1.20 [1.16-1.25]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: A 5 kg/m(2) higher BMI was associated with 60-120% higher renal mortality (HR 1.59 [1.27-1.99]).
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.

evidence.text: Excess mortality below 22.5 kg/m(2) is due mainly to smoking-related diseases.
evidence.source: Body-mass index and cause-specific mortality in 900 000 adults: collaborative analyses of 57 prospective studies.
